titleStmt.title: Letter from Fr Francis M. Shaw to Fr Provincial Thomas V. Nolan, 18 July 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Francis Shaw

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Francis M. Shaw (1881-1924) to Father Provincial Thomas V. Nolan (1867-1941) regarding his work on the hospital ship 'Delta' and the Catholics who he met there.Francis, or Frank, Shaw SJ was born in Ennis, county Clare. He was ordained on 31 July 1916 at Milltown Park, Dublin. Fr Shaw joined the war early in 1916, working in the 16th General Field Hospital at Le Tréporte. In 1917, possibly due to his nationalistic views, Fr Shaw was dispatched to India, and later to Mesopotamia.Fr. Thomas V. Nolan was the Provincial of the Irish Province of the Society of Jesus (1912-1922) and was a member of the Distribution Committee which looked after the welfare and distribution of the Belgian refugees who arrived in Ireland as a consequence of the First World War.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Archbishop's House, WODEHOUSE ROAD FORT. BOMBAY. 18 July 1917. My dear Fr Provincial, P.C. I hope you got my last
from Bombay with cheque enclosed.
(Â£50) It will be of more use to you
than to the Mediterranean fishes. I have just completed my first
trip on the Hospital Ship 'Delta'.
I had a good number of Catholics
on board coming back â Indians,
Burmans, Irish, English, West
Africans. It was very hot up
Gulf way, but the sea is as flat
as a pancake, and Mass is
possible everyday. I only lost
two Masses on the way up, and
I don't think I need have lost
them. However I thought it
better not to run the risk. I
asked you to tell Fr Farley I
had 20 Masses for him for
June. Please tell him I
have 20 also for July. These
numbers take into account the
4 suffrage masses for Fr J.
Maher & Br Mahon â I have
said 20 clear for him each
of the two months â or shall
have if God spares me to the
end of July. I gave Fr Hartigan a special
remembrance on 16th last. I
was not up the Gulf at the time. Fr O' Mahony's boat is on our
run also. They were anchored
near us for a few days, but
not near enough to communicate. He ought to be in Bombay today
or tomorrow. I sleep on the
ship, though the Fathers here
want me to stay here; but many
of my crew are Catholics and
they can have Mass on board
when I am there. The Indians
are very good Catholics â
especially the Goanese â
and these latter make up
the great bulk of the stewards
on boats out this way & on
P & O & British India Steamers.
I am fortunate in being on the
Hospital ship, above all being
so often here, as I can do a
lot of work in collecting sermon
and retreat matter. I get
an odd bit of news about
Ireland in various papers
but have had no letters from
home since I left. Two â
certainly one â recent mails from
England to these parts are at
the bottom of the sea. Any
of the last status news will be
more than welcome. I commend myself to your
Reverence's masses and prayers
and hope soon to be able to
lose myself again in the holy
obscurity of the Island of Saints. Francis M. Shaw S.J.
